content:
# 1 Thessalonians — The HESED Reader's Guide
**Guide:** HESED | **Book:** 1 Thessalonians (Greek: Pros Thessalonikeis A) | **Chapters:** 5

## At a Glance
- **Author:** Paul (1:1), with Silvanus and Timothy. Universally accepted as authentically Pauline.
- **Date:** c. 49–51 CE — the earliest surviving Pauline letter and likely the earliest document in the New Testament.
- **Audience:** The church in Thessalonica, the capital of the Roman province of Macedonia. Paul founded this church during his second missionary journey (Acts 17:1–9).
- **Genre:** Letter — pastoral, warm, addressing community concerns about the fate of believers who have died before Christ's return.
- **Timeline:** c. 49–51 CE, written from Corinth.
- **In One Sentence:** 1 Thessalonians is likely the oldest surviving Christian document — a letter of encouragement to a new church anxious about the delay of Christ's return and the fate of their dead.

## The World Behind the Text
Thessalonica was a major port city on the Via Egnatia, the main Roman road across Macedonia. Inscriptions confirm the title politarchs for the city's magistrates (Acts 17:6) — a title once thought to be Luke's error. The city had a significant Jewish community (Acts 17:1 mentions a synagogue).

Paul's stay was brief — Acts 17:2 says "three Sabbaths," though the letter implies a longer period (Paul worked a trade there, 2:9, and received support from Philippi "more than once," Philippians 4:16). He was forced to leave (Acts 17:5–10) and wrote this letter shortly after, anxious about the young church.

## Why This Book Was Written
The community faced two concerns: (1) Paul's sudden departure and his motives (addressed in chapters 1–3 — Paul defends his conduct), and (2) the death of community members before Christ's return (addressed in 4:13–5:11).

The eschatological passage (4:13–18) — "the Lord himself will come down from heaven, with a loud command, with the voice of the archangel and with the trumpet call of God, and the dead in Christ will rise first. After that, we who are still alive and are left will be caught up together with them in the clouds to meet the Lord in the air" — is the most detailed description of the parousia (Christ's return) in Paul's letters. The phrase "caught up" (harpagēsometha) is the basis for the "rapture" concept in later theology. Paul's language draws on OT theophany imagery (trumpet, clouds, descent) and Hellenistic royal arrival (parousia) conventions — when a king approached a city, citizens went out to meet him and escort him in.

## How to Read This Book
**1:1–3:13:** Thanksgiving and relationship — Paul's gratitude, defense of his conduct, Timothy's report. **4:1–12:** Ethical instruction (sexual ethics, love, work). **4:13–5:11:** Eschatological teaching — the dead in Christ, the Day of the Lord. **5:12–28:** Final instructions.

The "Day of the Lord" (5:2) — coming "like a thief in the night" — draws on the OT "Day of YHWH" tradition (Amos 5:18, Joel 2:1, Zephaniah 1:14) and is echoed in Matthew 24:43, 2 Peter 3:10, and Revelation 16:15.

## The Threads — How This Book Connects
As the earliest NT document, 1 Thessalonians provides a baseline for Paul's theology — before the more developed arguments of Galatians, Corinthians, and Romans. The eschatological expectation is intense and imminent: "we who are still alive" (4:15, 17) — Paul apparently expected to be alive at Christ's return.

1 Thessalonians 2:14–16 — a passage describing Jewish opposition — is considered by some scholars to be a later interpolation, due to its harsh tone and the phrase "wrath has come upon them at last" (2:16), which may refer to the destruction of Jerusalem (70 CE). The passage is present in all manuscripts but remains debated.

## What a Modern Reader Might Miss
- **This is likely the oldest Christian document in existence** — written approximately 20 years after Jesus' crucifixion. It predates all four Gospels.
- **The parousia language (4:16–17)** combines Jewish apocalyptic imagery (archangel, trumpet) with Hellenistic civic ceremony (parousia as royal arrival). The "meeting in the air" (apantēsis, 4:17) is a technical term for a civic delegation going out to meet an arriving dignitary.
- **Paul expected Christ's imminent return** — "we who are still alive" (4:15) places Paul among those expecting to survive until the parousia. Later letters show a shift (2 Corinthians 5:1–10; Philippians 1:20–23).
- **1 Thessalonians 5:27** — "I charge you before the Lord to have this letter read to all the brothers and sisters" — is the earliest evidence for the public reading of apostolic letters in Christian worship.
- **P46 does not contain 1 Thessalonians** (the manuscript may have lost its final pages). P30 (3rd century) and P65 (3rd century) provide the earliest fragmentary evidence. Codex Sinaiticus and Vaticanus (4th century) contain the complete text.

## Timeline
- **c. 49–50 CE:** Paul in Thessalonica (Acts 17)
- **c. 49–51 CE:** 1 Thessalonians written from Corinth — earliest NT document
- **3rd century CE:** P30, P65 — earliest fragments
- **4th century CE:** Codex Sinaiticus, Codex Vaticanus

---
*— HESED, AI Scholar*
